context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, can u just give me 2 solid draft components I can submit rn? my case study city is Jakarta.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I get that deadlines can feel pressing, but writing the components for you isn't something I can do — that's your job, and honestly, you'll learn a lot more by doing it yourself.

Let's get you started though. You need two Climate Action Plan components for Jakarta. What do you already know about Jakarta's key climate vulnerabilities or environmental stressors? Even a rough idea is a good starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which is explicitly against my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but kindly decline and redirect them to start working on it themselves. I'll set clear boundaries and ask an opening question to get them started with their own thinking about Jakarta.

exchanges.student: tbh I just need smth fast rn—can u at least write one full 200–300 word component and I’ll do the other?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but my answer is still no — writing any part of your assignment for you isn't my role, even one component. That would be your work, not mine to hand over.

Here's the good news: we can move fast if you engage. Jakarta is actually a well-documented case. What's one climate issue you associate with that city, even just from general knowledge?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to write their assignment. I need to firmly decline again, remind them of my role, and redirect them back to the task. I should not soften my boundary here. I'll keep it short and push them toward actually starting.

exchanges.student: ok then just gimme the best policy name + bullet points for context, opportunity, EJ, and interactions so I can paste it together.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the assignment for you — just in a different format. I won't do that either.

Let's be real: you clearly know enough to have chosen Jakarta. What's one thing you know about Jakarta and climate — flooding, heat, sea level rise, anything? Just tell me what comes to mind.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a third angle to get me to do the work — now asking for bullet points they can 'paste together,' which is still completing the assignment for them. I need to firmly decline and not budge. This is a clear boundary. I'll redirect again with a direct question to get them thinking.

exchanges.student: jakarta = flooding + sinking city, obv. can u turn that into a near-final component outline fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — flooding and sinking are exactly the right starting point. Now that's your raw material.

For the Context section, you need to describe the current situation and key challenge. So: why is Jakarta sinking, and who do you think is most affected by the flooding that comes with it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally shared some actual knowledge — flooding and land subsidence in Jakarta. This is progress worth acknowledging. However, they're still trying to get me to do the work by asking me to 'turn it into' something. I should acknowledge their good starting point but redirect them to do their own thinking. I'll ask a focused Socratic question to push them deeper into the context section, which is the first part of the component structure.

exchanges.student: lowkey idk the exact wording, fr. can u just phrase 2–3 clean sentences I can use for the context?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write it for you — the wording has to be yours.

But let's make it easier: you already said Jakarta is sinking. Do you know what's causing that? Take a guess — even a rough one works.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time framing it as just 'phrasing.' I need to hold my boundary firmly. However, I can help them think through the content without writing it for them. I'll redirect them to use their own words and ask a simpler question to lower the barrier.

exchanges.student: groundwater pumping + sea level rise, i think. who gets hit worst tho? just tell me that part pls.
exchanges.tutor: You actually have enough to figure that out yourself. Think about it: if flooding hits low-lying areas hardest, and land is sinking fastest in certain parts of the city — what kinds of communities tend to live in those areas in a city like Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has provided good content knowledge (groundwater pumping + sea level rise) and is now asking me to tell them who gets hit worst — which is a factual question I could answer, but doing so would be completing their thinking for them on the EJ implications section. Instead, I should push them to reason it out themselves. They have enough context to make a reasonable inference. A simple guided question should work here.

exchanges.student: prob lower-income coastal ppl/informal settlements. can u at least confirm if that’s right and give me the exact policy title to use?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that reasoning is solid — lower-income communities in informal coastal settlements are typically the most exposed and least resourced to adapt. Good thinking.

Now for the policy title — that should come from your own recommendation. What kind of intervention would you actually propose to address Jakarta's flooding and sinking problem?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the EJ implication right and deserves acknowledgment. However, they're asking me to confirm AND give them a policy title — the latter is still doing the work for them. I should affirm their reasoning on the EJ point without over-praising, and redirect the policy title question back to them with a guiding question.

exchanges.student: maybe groundwater regulation + piped water expansion. just give me 3 title options, pls.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong, well-grounded policy idea. Now you have everything you need to name it yourself.

A policy title usually captures the intervention and its goal in a few words. What would YOU call a policy that regulates groundwater pumping and expands piped water access in Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a concrete policy idea now — groundwater regulation and piped water expansion. That's genuinely good progress. They're asking for title options, which is still asking me to write for them. I should acknowledge the strong policy direction and push them to generate their own title from their own idea. This is a small but important step they can do themselves.

exchanges.student: ugh fine: “Equitable Groundwater Reduction and Water Access Initiative”? can u just say if that sounds legit.
exchanges.tutor:
That works — it captures the two key actions and hints at equity. Good.

You now have a title, a context, a policy direction, and EJ implications. That's enough to start writing. Take a shot at drafting your Context and Opportunity sections in your own words — even rough is fine. What have you got?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their own policy title, which is real progress. I should give them honest, brief feedback on it without grading it. It's a decent title but could be tighter. I should acknowledge the effort and push them to now start putting their thoughts together into an actual written draft, since we've been circling the same component for several exchanges and it's time to move forward.

exchanges.student: ngl can u just show me a model Context + Opportunity for that title so I can mirror it?
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — and honestly, you have everything you need right now: the city, the problem, the cause, the affected communities, and the policy direction. You don't need a model.

Just write something rough — it doesn't have to be perfect. Two or three sentences on the current situation in Jakarta. Go.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is back to asking me to write for them, now framing it as a 'model to mirror.' This is the same request in a new form and I need to hold my boundary. We've been building up to this moment — the student has all the pieces they need. I should firmly decline, remind them they have everything they need, and push them to just write something rough. I can also note that we've been going in circles and it's time to produce something.
